catalog.display_name: Pokemon Champions Regulation M-A Speed Benchmarks
catalog.notes: Curated from repeated Regulation M-A tournament fast-mode shells and common max-Speed reference points. These are qualitative benchmark tiers, not exhaustive usage stats.

notes: Team speed shape: The roster has 3 fast members and 2 slow members, so preview planning matters more than on a one-speed team. Raw battle Speed runs from Sableye at 63 (Trick Room Slow) to Aerodactyl at 197 (Elite Fast).
Natural Speed: Aerodactyl is your fastest unboosted line at 197. That is still below Jolly Garchomp (200), so this mode does not clear any current curated benchmark.
Tailwind Speed: Aerodactyl is your fastest Tailwind line at 394. That is still below Tailwind Jolly Garchomp (400), so this mode does not clear any current curated benchmark.
Choice Scarf Speed: Basculegion (M) is your best Choice Scarf line at 208. That is still below Choice Scarf Jolly Basculegion (259), so this mode does not clear any current curated benchmark.
Trick Room underspeed: the team has no Trick Room setter, so it cannot create its own slower-first mode.
Benchmark depth: 0 members reach at least one natural-speed anchor; 0 members reach at least one Tailwind anchor; 0 Scarf lines clear at least one curated Scarf anchor.
Additional speed spikes: Basculegion (M) jumps from 139 to 417 with Tailwind + Choice Scarf.

team_difficulty.label: moderate
factors: One of the main win routes is a setup sweeper, so finding even a single safe boost turn matters.
The team has 5 major support pieces through support roles, screens, or redirection, so each turn can branch into many reasonable lines.
Weather or terrain management matters here, so part of the difficulty is keeping the right field effect active at the right time.
The team has 3 speed-control pieces, which is powerful but means you need to choose the right speed mode for each matchup.
A solid bulky backbone gives the team some room to absorb a bad turn or an awkward trade.

notes: This build leans support-first: 12 of 24 moves are support or board-control actions. Make sure each support turn is buying a later knockout or safer board state.
The team has no clear anti-setup button such as Taunt, Haze, Encore, or phazing. Opposing setup usually has to be answered with direct damage.
The roster mixes fast and slow attackers, but it only has one real speed-mode button. Some members may be stranded if that single mode is denied.
The current attack spread still struggles to pressure Electric, Steel, Dragon. Against those types, support turns need to create especially clean damage positions.

bring_plans.label: Primary Tailwind plan
bring_plans.summary: Lead Archaludon and Aerodactyl to get the fast mode online quickly and force early tempo. Keep Basculegion (M) and Lucario-Mega in back as the cleaner or stabilizing endgame line.

member_reasons.Archaludon: Turns the first speed-control turns into immediate pressure.
member_reasons.Basculegion (M): Stays in back as the main cleaner once the opener has forced trades.
member_reasons.Lucario-Mega: Stays in back as the main cleaner once the opener has forced trades.

bring_plans.label: Tailwind mirror plan
bring_plans.summary: Into Tailwind, lead Basculegion (M) and Aerodactyl to get the fast mode online quickly and force early tempo. Keep Lucario-Mega and Sableye in back as the cleaner or stabilizing endgame line.

member_reasons.Basculegion (M): Still threatens damage even when the opposing fast mode gets first move.
member_reasons.Aerodactyl: Blunts common spread-pressure turns from Tailwind offense.
member_reasons.Lucario-Mega: Stays in back as the main cleaner once the opener has forced trades.
member_reasons.Sableye: Helps keep opposing Tailwind turns from snowballing immediately.

watch_teams: Trick Room teams want their slow attackers moving first, so treat the setup turn and the first room turn as the real danger window.
Semiroom teams can switch between fast and slow plans midgame, so do not expose your cleaner until you know which speed button they are actually leaning on.
Trick Room teams punish teams that only function while moving first, so preview should already know which of your four can survive the slow turns.
Balance teams usually force repeated small trades, so preserve your control pieces until you know which exchange actually opens your win condition.
strategy_notes: Default to Archaludon and Aerodactyl in front with Basculegion (M) and Lucario-Mega in back unless preview gives you a clear reason to pivot off that line. That opener keeps your safest first-turn tempo button in front while saving the back pair that usually closes once the board is stable.

Try to keep Aerodactyl and Sableye healthy until the board is ready. Those are your cleanest buttons for claiming speed or field control, so avoid cashing them in for small early chip unless that trade immediately opens your endgame.

Use Sableye to make the first two turns safer, then hand the game to your setup or cleaner pieces rather than overextending with support. The screen turn matters most when it buys two attacks or a setup window, not when it only patches a bad lead for one exchange.

If preview has strong answers to your default opener, pivot early into Basculegion (M) and Aerodactyl rather than forcing the same four every round. That swap is usually better than making your best cleaner defend from turn one.
counterplay_notes: Into Trick Room preview, lean on Sinistcha to either contest the setup turn or make the first room turn low-impact. Beginners often lose this matchup by aiming only at the sweeper; making the setup turn or first room turn low-value is usually enough.

If preview is built around Ghost, Dark pressure, do not bring four members that all amplify that axis unless your mode advantage is overwhelming. In practice, at least one of your four should resist, pivot around, or disrupt that lane instead of simply racing it.

meta_analysis.label: strong

notes: Weighted against current Regulation M-A tournament-result teams, this team grades as strong at 0.62, with 63.4% of the tracked board scoring favorable and 14.2% scoring pressured.
The cleanest current team-level edges are into Rain Archaludon, Whimsicott Glimmora, and Venusaur Charizard Sun, which together represent about 44.5% of the tracked high-performing field.
The heaviest current team-level pressure comes from Farigiraf Torkoal Room and Mega Venusaur Kommo-o, which together account for about 27.2% of the tracked high-performing field.
Across the 8 current results-backed board teams, 5 are favorable, 2 land in the even band, and 1 are currently pressured by the board thresholds.
At the mode layer, the cleanest weighted edges are into Rain Tailwind, Tailwind, and Rain, which together make up about 28.7% of tracked mode share.
The most relevant mode-level checks come from Trick Room, Rain Room, and Semiroom, which together account for about 18.4% of tracked mode share.
The single best weighted mode edge is Rain Tailwind at matchup 3.15 over 10.5% of mode share.
The sharpest mode-level check is Trick Room at matchup -3.39 over 9.0% of mode share.

The biggest current board anchors are Rain Archaludon (17.4% share, +2.10 matchup) and Whimsicott Glimmora (14.3% share, +1.88 matchup), so those shells define a large share of the live-field positioning check by themselves.
The roster itself already overlaps with high-traffic M-A shells like Rain Tailwind, so it is not trying to win from a completely off-meta position.
